CURRENT ATFAIRS.

We turned with amused anticipation to the ‘‘ Irish
Times’ ”’ leading article on the Home Rule Bill as
gketched out by Mr. Asquith. It isn’t good enough for
Alf Fox! We knew it would be that way. There are
too many brakes and restrictions in it! Alf says:—

““This is the boon which Ireland is to take in
exchange for her place in the councils of the Em-
pire. This perpetugl and harried subservience to
Imperial authority is the ‘self-government’ for
which Nationalist Ireland has made a thousand
sacrifices.”’

You would think it was a Tin Pike paper that you were,

reading.

The ““ Irish Times ” was in a great huiry to let the
cat out of the bag, or, in other words, to play the sup-
posed trump of fiscal autonomy. We doubt if it was
as shrewd as it would like to be in playing its hands so
soon, so crudely and so openly. It says:i—

‘“ Since the Bill withholds anything in the
nature of true legislative autonomy, Nationalists
will look eagerly for substantial compensation in
other directions. They will not find it in
the Bill. Ireland is to get a cerbain control
of her own taxation and expenditure, but genuine
fiscal control is carefully denied to her. The
cherished vision of fiscal autonomy has fled for
ever. We may play, to a certain extent, with our
own money, but the real and ultimate control of
national finance is kept in other hands.””

And again:—

** There is to be no Irish control of Customs and
Tixcise: the Government has definitely rejected the
recommendations of Sir Henry Primrose’s Com-
mittee to that effect. The Irish Parliament gets
just enough liberty to tax an industry out of exist-
ence, or to discriminate against a minority, but not
enough to malke Ireland the real mistress of her
own house.”’ :

The ‘‘ Irish Times ~—the bitter, anti-Irish and anti-
Catholic paper that it is—is too simple for anything,
though we suppose it will carry some weight with the
rather brainless, tame Catholics who take it in with
their milk every morning. It hopes that ‘‘ all educated
and patriotic Trishmen will make haste to repudiate it."”
Note the ‘“ educated ” and the ‘ patriotic "’ ; we are sure
that ““ educated " persons like ‘‘ Mike the Catholic ’
and patriot letter-writers like the ‘‘ Roman Catholic
Farmer,”” who cost the ““ Irish Times’’ so dearly, will
at once repudiate the Bill. ~The ‘‘ Irish Times *’ says:

‘* We do not know what Mr. Devlin’s Convention
will say to the third Home Rule Bill. We hope
and believe that all educated and patriotic Irishmen
will make haste to repudiate it.”’

The Bill is not good enough for the ‘‘ patriotic *.
““ Irish Times,”’ and this libeller of Catholics is not satis-
fied that under the Bill the hands of the bloody Papishes
could be kept off the throats of the Protestants.

““ The Bill’s safeguards for the religious and civil
liberties of minorities must be as illusory in prac-
tice as they are drastic on paper.”

We have not seen the Bill yet, but the statement of
Mr. Asquith and the speech of Mr. Redmond are to-
gether scarcely as convincing in the way of evidence to
us that the Bill, taken as a whole, is & good busjness
proposition as is the silly, unconvincing and inevitable
snarling and barking at it of the * Irish Times.” Here
is another quotation:—

““This Bill robs us of the Union, and gives us
none of the heroic possibilities of self-government.
It is & mean, suspicious, nation-killing measure.
For out present great position in the Empire it sub-
stibutes ‘a status of fiercely invigilated subordina-
tion. There is no room for the growth of any
national spirit within the narrow walls of Mr. As-

" quith’s huckstering statesmanship. He has driven
‘a hard bargain with Mr, Redmond, and has got the
better of him and of Ireland every time. ~ The
nominated Senate is an outrage on a democratic

Land League?

Constitution.' ~ We. wonder what Michael Davitt:
would havessaid of it if he:had been spared to vote
at the National Convention?.’’ * ”

We quote that for the quiet laughter of our readers.
In the end'it'dragsin the name of Davitt! What did
the- Irish-Times”’ say- of- Davitt-in the days-of the-

|

Haying finished with the *“ patriotic *’ paper of West-
moreland Street, we turn to the ‘‘rebel’’ sheet: of
D’Olier Street—for we must. not forget that the.rebel-
lious Ha’penny Dreadful took it as a libel and.an insult
that a statement was made in error that a Union Jack
floated over its premises. What does William Rebel
Murphy’s paper say? Politically we de not trust that
D’Olier Street ‘“rebel.””  The Bill appears to have
taken a lot of wind out of it, as it did out of screeching
William. We have seen that the “ Irish Times ™
rushed in and played the fiscal autonomy card at the
first round, but the Bill is a little too liberal for Murphy
the Rebel, whose paper is insulted at the false and base
suggestion that a Union Jack flapped over its premises.
The paper of Murphy the Rebel is constrained to say:—

“ Considering all the circumstances, we think
the Bill is not one to enthuse over, but the people
have no alternative but to accept it, though they
may not all be fully satisfied with it. Wa yield to
no Nationalist in our desire to see Ireland possess-
ing a Parliament in which all National affairs of
every description, including the power of levying
and collecting all the taxes contributed by the
country, could be controlled and managed by Irish-
men themselves. It is probably a correct descrip-
tion of this Bill to say that it gives three-quarters
of what we expected, and probably not so large a
share of what we demanded. It is better than a
half measure, but far short of a full measure. It
is not wholly satisfactory ; it is not thorough enough,
and it can hardly be regarded as final. It may
fairly be described as a moderate measure.’

Considering all I said and all I say

1 really think its time for me to hide,

The ice is breaking up on every side.

It is in its way amusing to contemplate William
Martin Murphy, ** the Rebel.” A Union Jack waved
over the upper storeys of the building in which the
““Independent ”’ offices are, and the Independent
was denounced on the mistaken assumption that H]e
‘“ Independent ”’ was responsible. And  how did
Murphy’s ““ Independent  take it ? They took it as
an insult and called for an apology! Murphy, who has
been walking like a tame terrier for years after English
royalty, blossomed out—at least the paper that he owns
did so—as an injured and maligned rebel! Of course
the only consistent attitude for a man of Murphy’s
stamp would have been to point out, if he liked, that
as a matter of fact the flag was not put out by him,
but that even if it had been, he being a runner after
English royalty and Dublin Castle for so many years,
would have no apology to make. But the ‘‘ Indepen-
dent ”’ was taken off its guard; and fearing for its ha’-
pennies, it trotted out as a ‘‘ rebel,’”” and took the sug-
gestion that it put out a Union Jack as a foul libel on
its rebellious self!

It is one of the lies of the Carrion Crows that the
Unionists in ‘‘ the South and West ’ have to lie very
ow. Manorhamilton is very much in the West, and
we have recently seen that almost as soon as a private
hotel or boarding house changed hands from a Protes-
tant to a Catholic that two ‘ Saved’’ bank clerks
promptly left it. Now we hear that one of the large
““ Saved " traders in Manorhamilton, who is living of
course largely on Nationalist custom, has canvassed for
the anti-Home Rule Fund. And yet according to the
ies prevailing the intelligent New Zealander would con-
clude that a “‘ poor persecuted ’’ Protestant in Manor-
hamilton ought to be afraid almost to be seen eating
meat on a Friday!

To-night (Thursday) a public meeting will be held in
the Town Hall, Dundalk, under the auspices of the
ocal Vigilance Committee. ~His Eminence the Car-
dinal Primate of All Ireland will preside. Some of
those in Dublin concerned in the crusade against dirty
papers are active, and developments are likely to take
place in the near future. There are some papers that
contain dirt in their advertisement columns that other-
wise may not be very objectionable. ~ Would it be
feasible to induce reputable advertisers to agree to
decline to adverbise in any paper that let its space to
objectionable things. That would be an effective way
of dealing with most Irish papers that depended-on
Irish advertisers, bul whether the English advertisers
could be induced to take up any stand, with a view to
weeding dirty advertisements out of papers, is a matter
upon which we cannot express a decided opinion. But
as dirty advertisements are only a small thing in many
papers that: publish them, if a combination of the big

‘We would direct the attention of Irish farmers to
the ‘courses in Agriculture to be held by the Depart-
ment of Agriculture during the year 1912-13.  Every
fa.rmer’g son should obtain particulars. of these courses
with a view to taking advantage of one or other of them.
Courses in Horticulture, Torestry, and Creamery
Management are also provided for students who desire
to specialise in these branches. Practical training in
farmmg_ is provided at the Agricultural Stations ab
Ballyhaise, County Cavan, and Clonakilty, County
Cork. A more advanced course in Agriculture is given
at the Albert Agricultural College, Glasnevin. The
fees at all these schools include board and lod ging, and
are so low in the case of farmer’s sons that they may be
described as almost nominal.  Very valuable scholar-
ships in connection with the Agricultural faculty of the
Royal College of Science, Dublin, are offered to young
men having sound, practical knowledge of farming,
gardening, dairying, or forestry. Scholarships are
renewable for three or four years to enable the holders
to pl}baiu, free of cost, the most advanced technical
training in these subjects, and'to qualify themselves for
appointment as instructors under the Department.

It will be remembered that a letter from Mus.
Cousins, Hon. Sec. of the so-called ‘‘ Irish Women’s
Franchise League,’”’ was seized by the English police
at the offices of the Women'’s Social and Political Union
in England. In the course of that letter Mrs. Cousins
explained or’excused the sending of only six Suffragettes
irom TIreland to the recent London demonstration, on
the ground that ‘“ a great deal of local militant work
will have to be done on the occasion of the National
Convention.”” The National Convention has got timely
warning from Mrs. Cousins to beware of the shrieking
sisterhood.  Mrs. Skeffington publicly announced that
as far as the Irish Women’s Franchise League was con-
cerned they were done with the Irish Party; she per-
sonally resigned from the world-rocking Yibs. Having
dissociated herself from the Irish Party, she announced
that she thought she might say that a new phase of
militancy would be entered upon if the Convention
finally denied them their right to representation under
the Home Rule Bill. It will be remembered that the
secrefary of the so-called ‘‘ Irish Women’s Suffrage
Federation”” sent an intimidatory letter to the mem-
bers of the Irish Party. So we have that-letter, Mrs.
Cousins’ letter seized by the London police, and Mrs.
Skeffington’s threats to the Convention. To give him
his due Skeffy has taken his place unreservedly amongst
the girls; poor old Oldham has made an ass of himself,

.-l)u'r. that was to be expected; Kettle wants to have it

both ways. He is apparently afraid of the Sufira-
gettes, and he would like to keep on terms with whab
he would call *‘the pawty.”” Intellect without adequate
character is a poor mixture. The Professor wrote to the
lady who foreshadowed ructions, entailing possible jail
for women, at the National Convention. e told her
in her capacity as Hon. Sec. of the ‘T Women's
Tranchise League,”” that he resigned his associateship
with ““ profound regret,” because that league declared
war on the Irish ‘‘Pawty.”” Then Kettle condemns some
of the ‘‘pawty’’ for their attitude towards the * Concilia-
tion Bill.”” “Well, of course, it is a pity that Kettle is
not, leader of the ““ pawty,”” and, in addition to that,
leader of the two hemispheres. — Brother Kettle—for
we believe he is a member of the A.O.IT.—tells Mis.

' THE LEADER.

Cousins, whose letter concerning the Convention is -
now notorious—that he will bring the Suffragette fac-
tion’s case before the Convention—we n]reufly lnow of
Mrs. Skeffington’s threat to the Convention in this
connection.  The Suffragettes have declared war on
the Irish Party, and Kettle is to champion the
Suffragettes at the Convention: TLet the Suffra-
gettes  henpeck their husbands; the Convention
should not allow itself to be henpecked. Home
Rule has its many and great difficulties; the Suffra-
gettes did their little worst at the Churchill meeting in
Belfast, though they do not appear to have done any-
thing at Bonar Law’s meeting ; they put out their sand-
wich women at the great Dublin Demonstration, and
now, with the aid of Kettle, these people who have
declared war on the Irish Party, who have threatened
the Convention, are to appeal to the Convention. The
situation is absurd. The question of votes for women
does not come in in the present critical struggle for
Home Rule. Votes for women is a new and debatable
proposition in these countries and Home Rule is an old
fight. As well and as reasonably might Kettle, Skeffy,
Cruso and Oldham attempt to make such a matter as
Anti-Vaccination or the Minimum Wage a question at
the Convention as for Kettle to attempt to bring votes
for women before that body. We have to get Home
Rule—and we haven’t got it yet. When we get it we
will have plenty of time and opportunity for discussing
votes for women. Plainly, Kettle’s purpose—no doubt
under fearful pressure from the sandwich women—is
out of order, and we have no doubt it will be so ruled.

KING WILLIAM ON ORANGEMEN.

2]

Scene : Before King William’s Statue, College Green.
Time : Home Rule Night.

(Enter two Orangemen.)

1st Or.—Popery, slavery, brass money and wooden

shoes are the order in the House of Commons to-
night.

2nd Or.—The British Parliament to-night is but the

slave market of Rome, in which the poor loyalists

of Ireland are offered up for sale to gloating Molly

. Maguires and plundering Papists of the United

Irish League.

1st Or.—And Asquith is the auctioneer in chief, who,
if he only spoke his mind, would simply say :—
Friends, rebels, Papists, lend me your ears.
We have on hands a marketful of slaves
All ready for the hammer. Oh, such beauties!
A very large and very choice assortment
Of Protestants and Presbyterians fat,
With here and.there a Methodist most plump,
Which we do offer at the lowest price
To every Romish cattledriver who
Has helped us into office.

2nd Or.—The partition of Poland was never carried out
with half the horrors that the passing of Home Rule
would cause in Ulster.

Oh, loyalty, thy triumph ceased awhile,
And greed, thy sister, ceased with thee to smile,
When Popish treason to the British crown
Let loose her rebels upon Portadown.
Hope for & season vanished in dismay,
And Freedom shrieked as Carson ran away.

1st Or.—Standing here before the statue of King Wil-
liam, T feel that such a dreadful calamity can never
come to pass.

Oh, William, of the Roman nose,
With Roman crown and Roman clothes,
How can you sit so calm and still
While Papists hail a Home Rule Bill.
Oh, from your ashes rise again

With Schomberg in your kingly train,
And, taking as your martial chiefs,
Ned Carson with a bag of briefs,

Jim Campbell, Craig, and all the lot,
Who itch to fire off shell and shot,
Lead on victorious o’er their foes,

A gallant host of Carrion Crows.

King W.—I do for long times von very great shame feel
dot I de shampion of dem tam blackguards, and
seurvy swashbuckling eaters of de leek, de Orange-
mans of Ireland, am named,

2nd Or.—The glorious, pious and immortal champion
of Protestantism and liberty speaks. Ah, I knew
that the desperate plight of his beloved admirers
would op~n his iron lips, and make him turn on his
horse. >

1st Or.-—Oh, William, leave this rebel town,

And take a trip to Portadown.

King W.—What for should I to Portadown go ?

2nd Or.—To give the boys there who are drilling a sight
of your conquering face.

King W.—I only vish dot I to dem could de sight of
von big rope give.

1st Or.—By the Boyne, King Billy has turned in his
grave and become a Home Ruler.

2nd Or.—He has got tarred with a Papist brush some-
how or other. Let him stay where he is.

King.—I here among de decent peoples vil stay. Be
off, and de Orangemen’s drill and art of war learn,
vich is how to stones at womans and sick mans
throw.  Nefar-changing lumps of ignorant dirt,
away from my sight quit. I of you am very seck.

(Exeunt Orangemen after throwing a couple of stones

at the King.)

Bonar Law said :— Ireland is not, and never *has
been, a nation.”” Further on he said that he did not.
ask that the minority in Ireland should exercise any
ascendancy over the majority, but they had a right to
ask, ** that a majority, because it is in Treland, should
nob dominate a minority, because it is also in Ireland.’’
What sort of drivel is that ? Apply it to England—a
minority in England should not have an ascendancy
over the majority ; a majority, because it is in Iingland,
should not dominate a minority because it is also in
England | «Then the only way out is to get some other
State—say Germany for choice—to undertake the
governing of lingland and let England send a few repre-
sentatives to Germany! This is the second speech of
Bonar Law’s that we have read, and we don’t think
much of him.

We did not read of any harmless Sundwich Suffers
parading about the Belfast meeting. Oh no; i only

We see that Bonar Law admitted our contention that
Trish Catholics are in a small minority of the entity
that counts. We have been at pains to point that out
recently, for the false and superficial view that we Irish
Catholics are a majority is prevalent, and Trish Papi
being rather tame, it is calculated to sap our self-esteem
if we are possessed by the false notion that we are a
majority, weak, trembling and afraid of a minorit
We are a majority in Ireland, but the entity that counts
is the entity of Great Britain and Ireland, and in that
entity we are a small minority. Referring to the Nation-
alists, Bonar Law said :— “They are in the main a
Catholic population in a Protestant country. *

Ranting journalists’ descriptions of our friends the
Sourfaces of the North-East are becoming wearisome.
The adjectives ““ stern ”” and * grim ” are doing great
services, but sure the poor chaps are'always grim and

‘‘IRISH AND WELSH.”

THE Welslr “National Insurance Health Commis-
5 sioners have taken a step which is likely to have
mmportant results. It is nothing more or less than the
official recognition of the Welsh language.

. Three months back Civil Servants of all grades were
informed by their departmental chiefs that applica-
tions would be received from them for appointments
undel: the new Insurance Act; but while there was no
mention of any special qualification being necessary
for appointments under the Irish, English and Scotch
Commissioners, a knowledge of the Welsh language was
mentioned as being essential for obtaining positions
under the Welsh Insurance Commissioners.

There are more Irish speakers in Ireland than there
are Welsh speakers in Wales. Our language is equal
to(\\’elsh from a linguistic, historical, and commercial
point of view. Why, then, cannot we demand that it
receive the same official recognition? It is the plain
duty of the Gaelic League, and all who support its
policy, to demand for Irish the same position given to
Welsh.

The immediate effect would be the appointment of
men under the Irish Insurance Commissioners fitted
by a knowledge of the Irish language and Irish history
for such positions and animated by a-desire to work
the Act for the benefit of the people of Ireland. The
effect on the schools, of Irish being a necessary subject
for positions under the Insurance Act, would be that
teachers and students alike would at once realise its
importance from a commercial point of view: the one
thing needed to convince the multitude that the Irish
langnage movement had succeeded at last in reaching
** the Land of Promise.”’

The Insurance Act is one of the few Acts of Parlia-
ment passed in the British House of Commons, the
administration of which has been placed in the hands
of Irishmen in whom the public have placed their con-
fidence; and'such being the case, it is not too much to
expect that if the claims of the Irish language were
properly pressed on them by the Gaelic League, that
they would accord a similar position to the Irish
language, as has been given to Welsh by the Welsh
Insurance Commissioners.  Once a Department has
got into an official groove it is difficult to effect a
change, for any change is likely to be opposed by official
interests. In this case both machinery, precedents,
and official grooves are in the making, and it is now
the chance of the Gaelic League to sce to it that the
atmosphere of the new Department shall be Irish from
the start, and its officials, men imbued with a know-
ledge and a love of Irish. Granted Home Rule comes
a precedent for all public departments will have been
created and a new era for the Irish language will have
arrived.

It cannot be too strongly urged on the Gaelic League
to be up and doing at the present juncture, The
Teague has fought stiff fights in the past, and has stift
fights before it, but if it succeeds in having Tvish offici-
ally recognised under the Insurance Act it will have
wori a victory which will have important consequences.
What Wales has done, surely Ircland can do.

N recent successive numbers of the LEADER tWo .

articles appeared under the heading ** Irish His-
tory,”” which are an unscrupulous attack on my History
of Ireland, and incidentally on myself. The writer 15
careful to conceal his identity lest his credentials might
be examined, and his purpose discovered, though there
is no difficulty in seeing that his purpose is to p_ush the
sule of another work und so injure the sale of mine.

With very few exceptions the published re-
views of my volumes have been favourable,
and have indeed often been flattering, and even
when fault was found, as fault can be [opl}d
with every book, it was done in no unkindly spirit.
And if this nameless assailant had only published his
contributions in the shape of a review, and in the
columns of some anti-clerical organ, I would have passed
it by with the remark that there was one truculent
Trishman who could neither be generous nor just. But,
unfortunately, his contributions found a home in &
paper which has done good work for Ireland; it is not a
review but purely and simply an attack; and it is only
right that notice should be taken of it. I have so far
refrained from doing so because my duties at this period
of the year left me little time, and because, in addition,
I was waiting till the whole weight of the attack had
been delivered. ITor the writer, having done the worst
he could himself, invited others to follow his example.

My offence is indeed a grave one. For 15 years I
devoted ev: spare moment of my life to the work of
writing a history of Ireland. The money that was none
too plentiful was spent on books and in ting scenes
famous in Irish history. The vacation which was badly
needed was spent in public.libraries. And often, after
an exacting day of other work, my historical labours
were prolonged far into the night. All this was done
in the hope of doing a little for Ireland, and not cer-
tainly to gratify an ignoble ambition. But according
to this writer it was all done to write ‘‘ a libel on the
life, manners, customs and laws of our ancestors; to
prove that the Irish were a race of omadhauns’; to
make ‘* wild and reckless statements’ which ‘ belie
our character and nation.”” In other words, it was all
done to calumniate the land in which I was born, and
to degrade the race to which I am proud to belong.
This was the work undertaken and carried through by
an Irish priest, sprung from the people and living among
them, who is bound to them by a thousand ties, and
who at close quarters sees and admires those lovable
qualities which have so often captivated the stranger,
and which endear the Irish people so much to the heart
of an Irish priest.

This shameful charge could never have come from. a
self-respecting Irish Catholic, after reading what I had
written. Nor would it come from a self-respecting Trish
Protestant; for I remember with gratitude how many
Protestants have helped with words of encouragement:
and even with offers of service, a promise, as it seems
to me, of that coming time when Protestant and Catho-
lic will be found working together for the good of the
old land that is dear to both. This charge, however,
could be made by a lineal des
Cawtholic of O’Connell’s days, who pretended to re-
spect the priest, but who in reality despised him, and
would, if he dared, have sent him to dine in the ser-
vants” hall. And this charge could also be appropriately
made by one of these hybrid Trishmen of a later day
who prefer pagan to Christian ideals, and who hold that
if an Irish peasant boy is to find favour with an Irish

board the liner ‘‘Cedric,” and discussing
whether Ireland would ever become a great and real
industrial centre. ‘‘ What occurs to me,” said M1
Smyth, of Philadelphia, “is that something might
happen if only science could harness the mighty ocean
tides that well up round Ireland’s shores, and dis-
tribute electric power all over the island.”” ‘‘That,”
said an Irish traveller, ‘“is a beautiful half dream,
half prophecy, but havdly constitutes an actual entry
in the ledger of to:day. The dreams of Monday are the
realities of Saturday.”” *‘ Yes,”’ answered Mr. Smyth,
““but how about Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday and
Triday?” *“1 wish,” said Father Ryan in the corner,
““ that we could induce our people to concentrate their
attention on what is actually being done in Ireland, so
as to develop that, instead of putting up dreams and
prophecies to be dispelled by ridicule or crushed by
common-sense.’”’  ‘“'Well,”” retorted Mr. Smyth, “1I
have been looking round me since I came home on
holiday from the States, and excepting for tweeds and
bacon, T have seen little indeed of development, and
the emigration is worse than ever.” ‘‘ Have you a
Rosary beads about you?'’ said Father -Ryan. ‘I
have, Father,”” he replied. ‘“Was it made in Ire-
land?”’ said Father Ryan. ‘‘Really, I do not know
where it was made,” he replied. *‘ There you are,”’
said Father Ryan; * look here af this one which I
bought on Monday in Dublin, made in Ireland of Irish
raw materials, by Irish-hands, from asmall factory,
employing between thirty and forty people.”” ‘‘ Well,
I never heard of that,’”” said Mr. Smyth, ‘‘ and it’s
pretty enough too, but what are the beads made of?”’
*“Try and guess,” responded Father Ryan, ‘‘you
ought to know, you use the material every day of your
life, and you are probably wearing it on you now.’’
‘“That’s a curious thing,” said Mr. Smyth, ‘T can-
not guess what it is, it secems hard, yet is it trans-
lucent.”” “It’s so hard,” said Father Ryan, ‘‘ that

If this be true,”” said Mr. Smyth, ‘‘ why don’t
these people advertise and make their goods known all
over the world?”' ““ For the simple reason,’’ remarked
Father Ryan, ‘ that Irishmen do not sufficiently sup-
port the industry to enable them to luy out money in
advertising, but seem to prefer buying rosaries from
l'lv'nncc, instead of those made in their own country.
What we have all got to do is to be voluntary tariff
rc{ormorsfnot spouting on platforms, but going each
one of us into shops and insisting on buying the work
of our awn people and giving employment in our own
country.”

*‘ Now,” remarked the Irish traveller, ““you are
talking common-sense. Nabions are not made by
spoelchus on platforms, resolutions at meetings or
hostile tariffs created by legislative enactments. They
are created by the good-will and practical daily per-
sistent action of the people themselves. But you
have not told us, Sir, what is the material of which
the beads are made?”’

*“ Well, Sir,” said Father Ryan, ‘‘ these beads are
made from the horns of Irish cattle. They are manu-
factured in Dublin. No doubt, it is a long and up-hill
path to get Irish shopkeepers, who are agents for
foreign goods, to stock a new article of home manufac-
ture; but, after all, if the people want the goods, the
shops will have to keep them, and here is an opportu-
nity of which every good Catholic ought to avail
himself.”” ““ T am much obliged to you,” said Mr.
Smyth, ‘“ for this information, and my first duty when
I land up home will be to give an order to these people
and lend them a hand to success.”” ‘I think,”” re-
marked Father Ryan, “‘ you could not do better, for
just let me tell you the story of the industry: An
Englishman comes over to Ireland, and after two or
three years residence takes off his coat and begins
working according to his lights at the industrial
development of the country. It so happens, he is an
antiquarian and has collected in past times old pressed
horn boxes and various ornaments and knick-knacks
in this delightful material. Well, he wants to revive
this horn work, so he comes along to the last of the
Irish comb-makers and asks for help and encourage-
ment. The last of the Irish comb-makers tells him
that there is no money in it, and that he had better
not risk any in it, for who wants snuff-boxes of pressed
horn?  This sets the Englishman thinking, and he
lights at last on the ingenious idea of making Rosary
beads out of horn. Rosary beads ARE wanted all over
Ireland. and throughout the Catholic world. So this
Englishman and the son of the last of the comb-makers
start off together qn an expedition to Sheffield to find
a press that will make horn beads. And so on and so
forth the story continues along a tale of ingenious
mechanical development and gradually expanding
employment of labour, so that to-day over forty girls
are paid decent wages weekly, and are comfortably
lioused in well-lighted and ventilated work-rooms. I
was thinking about these girls yesterday when the
captain took me below and showed me on board this
vessel six hundred under the age of twenty-five, whom
he had embarked at Queenstown to carry away from
Ireland to the United States. They have cost to rear
and educate, each one of them, between one and two
hundred pounds. They had no time or opportunity
to serve their own country, and just at the moment,
when they might become valuable citizens of the king-

dom of Ireland they are presented to the Government
of the United States to develop America, to increase

it seems to happen, in the Providence of God, to carry
their religion and ‘the genius of their race to the most:

back again in their native land. I remember, Sir, on a
remote farm in the Rockies, seeing the 'iather of an
Irish family, who had been home on a visit to Ireland.,
on the night of his return, unwrapping from a handlker-

County Tipperary, and placing his six little children,
one by one, upon this fragment of his native land, that
they might from their earliest youth have imbedded in
Jtheir hearts the idea that they had set their feet on a
real bit of old Ireland.”

The Trish traveller gazed sadly into the stove, remark-
ing that he feared it would be a long day before the
crowded emigrants im our great cities acquired that
instinctive love of country which seems bred and born
in the Trish race, and is a virtue, perhaps, in the civic
order, but, nevertheless, of immense value in days such
as these, when huge masses of people from all parts
of the world move to and fro from shore to shore, and
gradually dilute national integrity, involving a cosmo-
politan instinct that may prove of great worth, but is,
as yet, a vast experiment in comparison with the real
national life of past ages. It was this real national life
that enabled Japan to stand up against Russia; ﬂ:. is
real national life that breathes through the hlstonc_al
plays of William Shakespeare; it is real national life
that adorns the history of the Five Books of Moses,
and surely it was loss of this real mational life that
brought about the decline and fall of the Roman
Empire. A kind of reflective sadness fell upon the
company. Father Ryan opened his Office book, when
his eyes fell upon the words: ‘* Zdificans Jergsalem
Dominus : dispersiones Israelis congregabit. Qui sanat
contritos corde: et alligat contritiones corum.”

FARMERS AND THEIR RESPONSIBILITIES.

]’ AM glad to have drawn a comment from ‘‘Rusticus’’
on some sentences of Mr. Gill's lecture about

the responsibilities of the farmers. I know his criti-
cism is directed to Mr. Gill's statement, not to my
commonplace endorsement of it; but although the
editor has in great part anticipated what .would have
been my answer, I would like space for a word more.

““ Rusticus '’ seems to suggest that ‘‘ such a thing as
irresponsible ownership *’ never existed or could exist
anywhere, and therefore that a warning against the
danger of its growth is out of place. Bub he seems to
mean by ‘ irresponsibility,”’ ownership morally free
from all duties to others or to self.

In my adoption of Mr. Gill’s phrase I meant—as he
obviously did himself, and as does everyone who uses
it—the ownership which ignores or does not realise
that it has any moral duties to its neighbours in the use
of its property. Of this Ireland, unfortunately, experi-
enced a great deal from her landlords in the past, and
some of our new farmer proprietors may lapse into the
same disregard if their social conscience is not kept
sensitive by friendly reminder, and if the nation is not
alert to safeguard and promote the common interests
of all.

The country has need and right to be especially
watchful for the farmer’s fulfilment of his duties more
than for that of any other class, for the very reason
that, as ‘“ Rusticus "’ says, so overwhelming a propor-
tion of the wealth of Ireland is derived from the land,
and so many others of all classes are dependent on it.

the farmer in getting rid of them. The labourer must
show himself willing and able to make his work more
valuable to his employer if he wants to improve his
circumstances or leave any opportunity of a livelihood
for his family at home. But the first step should be
taken by the farmer to establish more mutually service-
able relations by offering an improvement in wages for

It is certainly not an increase of publicans I desire
to see, but of farm labourers and rural tradesmen whom
tillage farming employs. It is lamentable what an
amount of their earnings some labourers, chiefly ‘ ser-
vant boys,”’ and some tradesmen and some farmers,

the country are not generally of a drinking class, and a
large increase in their numbers would not, I hope, be
much gain to the publican, but would chiefly serve
themselves and the nation. Does ‘“ Rusticus ' really
see no national advantage in the country’s preserving
and increasing its population ?

Any fears of the creed of socialism making progress
in TIreland outside of the large cities is grotesque. It
is, perhaps, over individualistic we are—Ilabourers and

But if anything could foster sympathy with its prin-
ciples it would be the tendency of some people to apply
this name in condemnation or alarm to any suggestion

providing for public needs by progressive taxation.

Of such a kind is the ridiculous remark of

where in a discussion, on the too literary education

said:—‘ It was boing taught in cerfain schools of

State would take steps to see that the land of the
country was utilised in the best interests of the people
of the country,”’ :

To_ this the Bishop replied :—'‘No.. There is no
socialism about it at all. These men say that where
population begins to press on the means of subsistence
in any country the State has a right to see that the land
of the country should be' utilised so as to ensure the
best possible results in the way of produce.’’

The natural comment of the extreme individualistic
point of view was thereupon made by another mem-
ber:—“T do not think a farmer could be compelled
to use a farm except as he thought fit for his own pur-
poses.””

His lordship said ‘““he was only telling them what
‘he read. He thought a man could be compelled by
taxation to adopt tillage as against pasturage if it were
necessary for the subsistence of the community and
not against the interests of his own family. A man’s’
family had the first claim. The theory to which he
was referving was that the day would soon come in
- country when farmers would be compelled, in the
of the community, to pay more attention to
productivity than to profit.”

However, with the prices like those of the past
season for corn and fat cattle, and with the informa-
tion which the Department’s schemes and experiments
supply of the most profitable methods of cropping, and
most of all with the growing spirit of self-reliance,
nationality, and, I trust, of responsibility, the farmers
will need no pressure of public opinion to induce them
to malke the very best of their industry.

THE ANNUAL SERMON
For the Support of the Above Institution
Will be Preached by the
REV. M. HICKEY, D.D., D.Ph.,

Holy Cross College, Clonliffe.

This Institution is for the Children of respectable
parvents, or Orphans who are unprotected, or whose
Guardians wish to have them trained to industry. The
number is at present one hundred and ten, who, during
their residence in this Establishment, ave provided for
in every respect.

The Sisters of Charity beg to assure the Benefactors
.of the Institution that since its commencement the
inmates have ever considered it their duty to pray daily
for their Benefactors; besides ‘which fifty-two Masses
are offered in the course of the year for their Benefac-
tors, living and dead, and on receiving a Legacy, or
being told that such has been willed to the Institution,
the Holy Sacrifice is offered for the Bequeather, regu-
lations which will continue to be religiously adhered to.

Donations will be gratefully received by His Grace
the Archbishop ; the Very Rev. Canon Burke, P.P.; the
Rev. Preacher; Edmund Sweetman, Esq., Longtown;
or by the Superioress, St. Mary’s, Stanhope Street.
